data.title: Takeda: Positioned To Tackle Future Clinical Demand In A Highly Regulated Sector
data.description:
Intro: A Japanese Pharma Giant Trading in U.S., With Positive Recent News

Just in time for what I call "follow-up Friday," I'm following up on a major global pharma stock I covered back in March, who has some interesting news lately that may interest investors.

Takeda Pharmaceutical Company Limited (NYSE:TAK, TYO: 4502), the Japan-based firm that trades in the U.S. under an ADR, recently announced that "data from two phase 3 trials on oveporexton for narcolepsy type 1 met all of its primary and secondary endpoints," as reported by Seeking Alpha news desk.

In a nutshell, it means that item in its drug pipeline is moving along, and as I've said in several articles on pharma stocks a robust pipeline is relevant for future growth.

Otherwise, this firm is known for solutions in the "areas of gastroenterology, rare diseases, plasma derived therapies, immunology, oncology, and neuroscience," according to its SA profile.

Although I often boast about my prior Buy ratings leading to price jumps, in the interest of being fair and balanced this particular stock I rated in March actually is slightly down now by -1.87% (as of pre-market on Thursday), after a series of ups and downs, so today I'll be re-exploring this one again and including price targets as part of the discussion this time.

Thesis: Invest In Future Clinical Needs That Just Keep Growing, As Does This Firm

My thesis today argues that Takeda continues to make an attractive long-term play, as its active pipeline works to meet future clinical needs, while the firm has been able to be a cashflow grower and with a low leverage risk. However, I think where it loses points on being a strong buy is weaker profit margins than some peers and its lack of steady dividend growth.

My rating table below shows how I arrived at this conclusion, the 6 rating categories and their weighted scores, and the average score overall which determined my rating.

I gave greater weight to two categories that really stuck out for me, top-line revenue growth potential and balance sheet risk.

Each of the following sections discusses these 6 rating categories, with a focus when possible on what could drive future upside and downside for this stock.

A Sector That Could Benefit From Continuing Demand For Niche Clinical Therapies

Like I said in my original article, on a macro and industry level a key factor that can drive future upside is rising demand for certain therapies, and I pointed to data forecasting a rise in prevalence of IBD (inflammatory bowel disease), for instance, a clinical segment that Takeda tackles with its ENTYVIO product, which also has won approval from European regulators too.

Besides those clinical segments I already mentioned in that article, today we know that Takeda is active in the narcolepsy segment to bring future therapies to market, saying days ago in a release that "currently, the standard of care is limited to symptomatic therapies that may only partially address some of the symptoms people face."

I think this clinical segment could be a driver of future upside, considering research firms like Canada/India based Precedence Research (who boasts clients like Morgan Stanley and PwC on its website) has said in a 2024 study that the global narcolepsy therapeutics market size "is anticipated to touch around USD 6.9 billion by 2032, expanding at a CAGR of 7.70% from 2023 to 2032."

As you can see from the visual above, there is expected steady market growth in this segment.

Another potential future upside in this sector in finding solutions in the oncology, or cancer-care space. Takeda's pipeline "focuses on thoracic, gastrointestinal and hematologic cancers," according to its website.

Data site Statista, for example, expects the worldwide market for oncology drugs "is likely to experience an annual growth rate (CAGR 2025-2030) of 6.25%, resulting in a market volume of US$294.01bn by 2030."

Further, I would point out from that Statista research the following trend:

There is also a growing trend towards the use of biosimilars, which are less expensive versions of existing biologic drugs. This trend is being driven by the need to control costs, as well as by increased competition from generic drug manufacturers.

Such macro factors, I believe, could continue to create opportunities for established firms like Takeda who already operate globally, despite being based in Japan, as they are not limited to their home country.

The factors above deserve some bullishness on this stock further.

However, this space also faces competition from peers who are also trying to get their therapies approved by regulators and to grow their pipeline across multiple clinical segments. So, for peer comparisons today I picked 4 other global pharma stocks: Pfizer (PFE), Merck (MRK), Novartis (NVS), and Teva (TEVA).

Upside Potential From Active Pipeline, As Well As Momentum From Existing Drug Portfolio

Since my March rating, this firm has had its most recent earnings release on May 8th, and this article today can be sort of a preview ahead of the next one on July 30th. Note that the quarter ending March was the end of the company's fiscal 2024.

We can see from their income statement that revenue grew on a YoY basis in the quarter ending March, and from a future view I would highlight that fwd revenue growth is expected to be over +100%, far above the sector average, which I think should get the attention of investors and analysts, creating upside potential.

From one of the quarterly presentations in May, I want to highlight something that can drive future revenue growth if they get approved for use, and those are NMEs (new molecular entities). According to the FDA, "these products frequently provide important new therapies for patients."

Here is how the firm's clinical trials pipeline is looking as of May, according to the investor presentation (pg 2), with 3 new NMEs as well as lifecycle management of existing products, which may be seeking approval for new clinical indications and so on:

I believe such a robust pipeline of trials is important in this competitive space, since some of those may or may not lead to regulatory approval. However, future potential drug approvals may not drive revenue in 2025, however those approved in fiscal 2024 could, so let's take a closer look.

From its last earnings presentation (pg25), an upside factor for driving 2025 /2026 revenue could be driven by key regulatory approvals in fiscal 2024 as follows:

Notable here is that the Takeda brand has penetrated multiple geographies (U.S., Europe, Asia), who each have their own local regulatory agencies which require approval separately for each market, and that is a notable achievement to grow the business further, along with increasing its globally diverse revenue portfolio.

I would also point out there is existing revenue momentum in the majority of Takeda's clinical segments, as we can see from YoY revenue growth data in their earnings presentation (pg 3):

So, I remain bullish on the category of top-line revenue growth potential, both because of the future potential of this firm's pipeline as well as recent approvals and its YoY momentum across multiple segments, showing a proven demand.

Profit Margins Tell A Mixed Story, But Cost Efficiency Gains Are Underway

Moving along, we talked about revenue drivers but now let's consider key margins that can impact the bottom line going forward.

As an analyst, I am looking for an EBITDA margin that meets or beats the sector average, and this one does, as Takeda is at nearly 27% vs the sector at around 8%. I mention this margin as an indicator of core business efficiency.

The net income margin, which can impact the reported net income, but also could be impacted by depreciation expense which is not tied to core business operations, also impresses with a margin of +2.3% vs the sector avg of -1.82%.

However, although Takeda's profit margins impress vs the sector, on a more granular level when compared to the peer group of big pharma stocks I selected below, it gets beaten on both margins by brands like Pfizer, Merck, and Novartis, too:

I think such comparison is relevant for investors who put more weight on profit margins than perhaps revenue growth, and are picking among several pharma stocks to add to their portfolio. In this specific metric, we can see the 3 stocks that are a better choice than Takeda, and more efficient it seems at turning revenue into actual profits.

Investors may also be hesitant about this stock considering it reported a net loss in the quarter ending March, and 3 of the last 7 quarters saw net losses reported.

At the same time, though, I like to mention any company initiatives to improve cost efficiency so notable along those lines are that Takeda reported in its investor presentation (pg 12) that its "efficiency program captured JPY 200B annualized savings to date."

I've written often about the role of AI in driving productivity in some areas, and this firm is no different, as that same presentation boasts that the firm is "leveraging data and AI across the entire value chain to create value, including in manufacturing, plasma collection, R&D, sales & marketing, G&A."

So, considering the multiple points made above such as this firm beating its sector average on margins but not key pharma peers, posting several losing quarters but also being committed to a cost-efficiency strategy, I think it is fair to give it a hold rating in this category of earnings growth potential.

D/E and Covered Ratio Paint Picture Of Attractive Balance Sheet Risk

We've come to the part focused on balance sheet risk, and here are some points that I think are relevant about Takeda.

One element of balance sheet risk is how much debt there is in relation to the company's equity, so I did a comparison of Takeda's D/E vs. peers:

As the data above shows, Takeda has a D/E of 0.65 which is the lowest in this peer group, but also has not climbed much in the 3 year period shown, while peers like Teva have a significantly higher debt to equity.

It paints the picture of Takeda having less reliance on debt leverage than some similar firms, and I consider that a positive upside.

In terms of recent credit ratings from major agencies, I found just limited info on Takeda, with its own website showing investment-grade ratings from Moody's and S&P but based on 2023 ratings.

So, to add a more recent metric to this balance sheet risk story, I picked the current ratio.

While opinions may vary, as a benchmark I used what investor site Investopedia said and that is

"a current ratio that is in line with the industry average or slightly higher is generally considered acceptable. A current ratio that is lower than the industry average may indicate a higher risk of financial distress or default by the company."

If you consider the peer comparison below, the average current ratio for all 5 peers is 1.10, so Takeda is only slightly below this peer average with a current ratio of 1.01, or about 8% below average which falls in line with the benchmark I selected above.

Based on the factors discussed above, with this firm having an acceptable current ratio and the lowest D/E among peers, I am inclined to say buy when it comes to its balance sheet risk, as I think it will be in a more favorable position if it needs to seek future debt financing, than a firm which is much more leveraged right now.

A Semi-Annual Dividend With Nice Yield But Lack of Proven Growth, Despite Cashflow Growth

In this section, let's consider some points about this stock's dividends, and what factors I place greater weight on.

If you are a dividend investor that places more weight on higher-yield stocks, consider the yield is around 4.5% and much higher than its sector average, however it is not a monthly or quarterly payout but a semi-annual one, so to me that is a slight negative.

If you put more weight on dividend growth and not on yield, as I have been doing lately, then the stock does not make my list of dividend ideas.

Consider that its 3, 5, and 10 year dividend CAGR is negative. For instance, its 10 year dividend CAGR is -1.11% vs the sector average of +7.02%.

Here is how that looks visually, which is not positive for investors looking for a proven dividend growth:

To add an additional angle to this story, it does not seem the problem is one of cashflow but rather that this is a very research and capital-intensive business that allocates a lot of extra capital towards that effort. As the firm said in its quarterly earnings commentary (pg 15),

"with respect to 'invest in growth drivers', Takeda makes strategic investments in internal and external opportunities to enhance its pipeline, new product launches, and plasma-derived therapies."

If you consider the cashflow picture it has been one of growth. Operating cashflow has grown almost +48% YoY, with forward growth expected at +16%, higher than the sector average, according to Seeking Alpha data.

Also, if you consider the cashflow statement and the quarter ending March, even though the company reported a net loss it used depreciation to make operating cashflow positive, while not taking on any new short or long-term debt financing that quarter to raise cash.

As I've written before, I have a greater preference for companies who can sustain their dividend through operating cashflow rather than raising debt, and that does boost my rating somewhat but nevertheless it has not shown it can steadily grow its dividend so I am giving it a low hold rating in this section.

Valued Just Below The Sector, While Presenting A Modest Future Upside Potential

In this section, as in recent articles, I'll be combining the topic of a forward-looking valuation metric with that of future price targets.

My valuation metric of choice, therefore, is the fwd P/E ratio (GAAP-based) which is 22.07, mostly in line with the sector average of 23.52, or a roughly 6% variation.

This tells me the market mostly expects this stock's future earnings to perform relatively in line with the rest of the big pharma sector. I think this may be slightly too optimistic and I was expecting a somewhat lower multiple, considering the profit margins compared to peers, that I mentioned earlier, and the net loss in the last quarterly result.

When plugging in that multiple in the table below, along with the analyst consensus EPS estimate for fiscal year ending March 2026, as well as considering average consensus targets showing on Seeking Alpha and the Wall St. Journal, I get the results below:

So, when averaging my own price target of $14.79, together with the consensus targets of $16.99 and $17.08, the avg target price is $16.29, which could provide a nearly +11% upside to Thursday's pre-market price of $14.72.

Here is how that future price (shown by blue arrow) could look on the price chart, compared to the current 200-day SMA and 5 year high/low:

Based on the forecast above, and the expected +11% upside or so, as well as this stock trading at a slightly lower multiple than its sector, I will go ahead and call this a buy opportunity in respect to future price expectations.

Rating Decision: Buy

From the holistic evidence above, a case can be made that this stock is a buy. In the next section, though, I want to mention a risk factor that could pose a challenge to my bullish sentiment, and not already included in my rating score.

Additional Risks Beyond My Thesis: FDA Disapprovals

One risk that can impact this firm, not already talked about in this article, is that even though I mentioned favorable results in its narcolepsy drug moving through the pipeline, the opposite can also happen with new drugs in the pipeline, where they get rejected, and that is the regulatory risk that can occur after a vast amount of capital has been invested in research and development.

Such rejections can also drive future downside risk for the stock and its price.

For instance, in December, MedCentral reported that

"the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) rejected Lexicon Pharmeceuticals (LXRX) new drug application for sotagliflozin (Zynquista)."

You can see that the December decision certainly did not help Lexicon's stock price, which remained well below its 200-day SMA until summertime.

Conclusion: Buy The Long-Term Growth Potential, At Low Balance Sheet Risk

To conclude this research article today, in the spirit of what I call "Follow-up Friday," my follow-up determined that this stock remains a buy opportunity, even though I applied additional angles not considered in my original rating.

Where this stock wins for me is the long-term role it will play to meet clinical demand for safe and effective therapies across multiple clinical segments through its diverse and active pipeline, while also proving it can grow operating cashflow and keep leverage risk low. However, it could use improvement in its profit margins and making the case as a dividend grower.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: EPR Properties: It's Time To Say Goodbye (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
My first article on Seeking Alpha was about EPR Properties (NYSE:EPR) back in Nov. last year. The stock is up 35% since that write-up and when you include all dividends, it should be around the 40% mark.

I respect my readers and followers a lot, so telling you about [big] position swaps or exits, feels right. Today I will write such a piece, because I've been aggressively selling my EPR Properties position in order to expand or initiate [new] positions.

In this relatively short piece I will share my rationale behind the 84% sale of one of my oldest holdings, initiated around 2020 - 2021. I've made great returns on this stock and at times, the position size equaled around 35 - 40% of my total portfolio. But I'm still proud, since my ADMA Biologics holding once made up 85% of my portfolio.

But no, I'm no gambler [I believe?] since I've always measured our [my wife and me] total stock investments against our combined paychecks. As our portfolio grows, a more disciplined approach towards position sizing becomes more and more important.

Valuation gap has shrunken

I've bashed "Mr. Market" a lot in recent articles, since I believe that many investors [big and small] are overvaluing more safe REITs/companies and undervaluing more leveraged [and therefore riskier] REITs/companies.

Even though some authors rated EPR a "sell" during all of my holding years, I've never bought into the "theatres are dead so EPR is a bad choice". Why? Well, industries adapt to changing consumer behaviors and humans are social creatures that prefer experiences together. I trusted management in buying back depressed shares during the COVID pandemic, preserving shareholder value and even maintaining full financial resilience during full lockdowns that hammered cashflow. My trust in EPR's management is huge, but the stock has become to expensive for me personally, since I'm a contrarian investor with a high risk-appetite. Not irrationally high, but still a lot higher than most investors.

Anyways, when looking at the last 5 years, the current P/AFFO ratio of around 12x is a lot higher compared to Mr. Market's 5-year historic valuation, coming in at a blended 8.71x. When looking back 18 years, the current valuation is more or less in line with the blended historic trading range.

If we, arbitrarily, put the Q2 2025 AFFO [up $0.02 since Q1 2025] at around $1.23, you will get an annualized $4.92 in AFFO, or a P/AFFO of around 12.20x taking a $60 share price.

Opportunity costs matter

I believe in the absolute value of EPR Properties. The company is de-risking its portfolio by focusing new investments on the non-theater part of the portfolio, reducing its concentration risk. With 38% of the current portfolio in theatres, a good move from management. In my latest piece on EPR [I've copied the important bit right below] I've highlighted management's focus on steering the company away from [over]concentration in one segment of the experiential industry.

The market seems to be catching on when it comes to the intrinsic value of EPR Properties. Especially the portfolio de-risking that is taking place since COVID-19, finally seems to be getting the love it deserves from Mr. Market.

But this creates a problem for me, because the relative valuation is getting unattractive right now. When I started covering Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) and Brandywine Realty Trust [two of my highest conviction picks right now] EPR Properties' share price was climbing while at the same time the other two REITs were tanking.

At the time of writing, you can see that the discrepancy in valuation between EPR and both ARE and BDN has increased a lot. I believe that Mr. Market is not [yet] seeing the value in BDN's underlying and expanding portfolio, while at the same time Mr. Market is overreacting to the oversupply and current "anti-science" sentiment coming from the current administration. At the same time, EPR Properties is doing just fine, but from a relative valuation standpoint, I see more value in the coming years from taking a larger position in the more beaten down REIT stocks.

If you're interested, feel free to read my articles on both ARE [link] and BDN [link]. It's important to understand that ARE is concentrated in the life science part of the REIT market, while BDN is focusing on mixed-use assets that focus on office, residential and life science assets, among others. Both REITs have A-tier assets in their portfolio.

Should you sell? It all depends..

Do you like a conservatively leveraged REIT that is paying you a well-covered +6% dividend yield that could increase by around + 3.5%? [based on last years increase], then you might like EPR Properties.

But if you have a higher risk appetite and you buy into [my] strong buy thesis based on the argument of mean reversion and Mr. Market's bipolar mood swings, Alexandria Real Estate and/or Brandywine Realty Trust are worth taking a look at. Both for different reasons and related risks, so please read my articles [but preferably other's too] before considering.

In this turbulent market, mispricing from a historic valuation perspective is vital for me. I buy into REITs that are trading at depressed valuations, waiting for Mr. Market's re-appreciation. EPR Properties no longer meets this important investment criterion.

I will certainly keep EPR Properties on my watchlist, waiting for a possible pull-back. My remaining 16.4% of EPR shares [164 shares] will probably be sold in the coming weeks. I will keep my followers updated when I do.

Goodbye my loverGoodbye my friendYou have been the oneYou have been the one for me

- James Blunt

Until we meet again.

data.title: Udemy: Consumer Interest And Enterprise Retention Are Falling
data.description:
Udemy, Inc. (NASDAQ:UDMY) operates a marketplace for learning skills. The platform has courses on a wide range of topics, ranging from business, accounting, software skills, personal development, fitness, and other categories.

After an IPO in late 2021, the stock hasn’t had a favorable trajectory. Udemy’s growth has fallen short of expectations. While Udemy is trying to grow through signing a growing number of enterprise customers, Udemy’s underlying performance signals a concerning growth trajectory. The company's improving profitability isn't enough to make Udemy attractive.

Udemy’s Growth Is Faltering

Udemy’s growth story has been disappointing after the company’s IPO during the Covid pandemic. Udemy was even previously thought to reach well over $2 billion in revenues by 2029, but analysts have had to lower expectations as Udemy hasn’t attracted growing interest in its platform. Currently, Udemy guides 2025 revenues to reach just $772-794 million, down by -0.5% from 2024 at the mid-point.

In the past few quarters, Udemy’s growth deceleration has been especially noticeable. The company still achieved good 15.9% growth in 2023, but growth has continuously decelerated into just a low single-digit level in Q1. The current guidance suggests continued deceleration into a revenue decline, and Udemy’s upcoming July 30th Q2 report is guided to show $195-199 million in revenues at a very shy 1.3% mid-point growth.

Underneath the concerning deceleration trend, Udemy’s organic web traffic is faltering as consumers aren’t interested in the platform. Meanwhile the company continues to spend a massive amount on marketing to acquire customers, including $343 million in 2024 alone, organic traffic to the website peaked back in July 2022 and has continued to fall consistently since. Udemy’s consumer segment revenues declined by -8.3% in Q1 to $73 million, reflecting a lower number of buyers and weaker revenues per purchase.

Udemy has tried to shift its focus increasingly towards the enterprise segment, providing more predictable subscription-based revenues to the company. Some performance indicators suggest the move to have been a success – the segment’s revenue grew by 8.6% in Q1 to $128 million alongside similar ARR growth. Total business customers grew by 7.1% to a total of 17216, including a good number of large customers. Yet, the enterprise segment is also showing signs of deceleration. Meanwhile the number of business customers has continued to grow consistently, the net retention rate has fallen to 96% from 106% in Q3’23. A similar trajectory in upcoming quarters, which Udemy’s 2025 guidance suggests, could turn the enterprise segment towards a revenue decline as well with older business customers seeming to leave the platform at an increasing rate.

Ultimately, the focus lies in Udemy’s ability to reinvigorate customer interest – Udemy clearly isn’t able to maintain revenues with the current offering. The company has put resources towards AI development in the platform, but its relevance to customers is uncertain. Further, the competitive landscape seems to pressure Udemy as for example Coursera (COUR) has grown above Udemy’s pace. The current business momentum doesn’t give much faith in a growth reacceleration.

Udemy Is Pulling the Profitability Lever

Meanwhile Udemy’s growth is decelerating, the company is pulling the profitability lever. Udemy guides for 2025 adjusted EBITDA of $77-87 million, up significantly from $43 million in 2024 despite the company’s slow revenue trajectory. The guidance follows Udemy’s previous improvements in EBITDA through the 2022-2024 period already. Udemy is rapidly cutting its operating expenses to improve profitability – the company has recently started to cut marketing expenses and R&D expenses have seen a sharp cut during the past two quarters. With more rigor over capital spending, Udemy is looking to reach healthy cash flow margins as well.

Notably, adjusted EBITDA is still quite a misleading metric – the metric adjusts stock-based compensation, of which Udemy has $85 million over the past twelve months. Such high compensation rapidly dilutes current shareholders as Udemy’s market capitalization stands at just shy of a billion at the time of writing. Healthy EBITDA enables healthy cash flow, but current shareholders look to hold a shrinking share of soon shrinking revenues.

Udemy’s Valuation Still Assumes Too Much

I built a discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Udemy’s stock. While a single scenario is challenging to model given Udemy’s uncertain revenue direction, I modeled a scenario in which growth slightly reaccelerates. Through the 2024-2034 period, I estimate a total revenue CAGR of 2.5% with 2% perpetual growth afterwards.

With Udemy’s focus on profitability, I estimate the EBITDA margin to reach a 13% level from 2028 forward. The assumption signals a good cost control meanwhile Udemy still manages to slightly improve growth. I estimate Udemy to convert 75% of EBITDA into free cash flow.

I have added a rough 30% as dilution to Udemy’s outstanding shares due to the company’s stock-based compensation.

The estimates put Udemy’s fair value at $5.59, 18% below the stock price at the time of writing – even with slightly improved growth, I don’t believe Udemy to be a worthwhile investment. The company would need to accelerate growth significantly, of which there are no current signs. As such, the stock provides a weak risk-to-reward even after the significant post-IPO decline.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 13.15%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I don’t expect Udemy to draw any debt. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.49% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the US. Yahoo Finance estimates Udemy’s beta at 1.81. With a liquidity premium of 0.3%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 13.15%.

Takeaway

Udemy’s growth is decelerating and is quickly approaching negative levels in 2025. Consumer interest in the platform has fallen, and Udemy’s enterprise focus is also flashing concerning signs in the retention rate. The company is focusing on increasing profitability, and while the cash flow outlook is solid, Udemy’s high stock-based compensation dilutes the investment. I believe the stock to carry a weak risk-to-reward, and as such, I initiate Udemy at a Sell rating.

data.title: BSV: Stability, Half-Term And Liquidity
data.description:
Introduction

In today's article, we will analyse the Vanguard Short-Term Bond (NYSEARCA:BSV) exchange-traded fund investment vehicle. This fund seeks to replicate the performance of the Bloomberg U.S. 1–5 Year Government/Credit Float Adjusted Index, which comprises investment-grade government and corporate bonds with maturities of 1 to 5 years. The fund operates under the umbrella of the investment manager Vanguard. The fund was founded in 2007 and currently has USD 37 billion in assets under management.

The fund under analysis employs passive management, which it carries out by sampling the index mentioned above. The fund selects a representative sample of the basket of bonds held by the index in order to replicate its returns and associated risks. Of the fund's total capital, it invests at least 80% of the total in this associated sample, leaving 20% in liquidity or in hedging operations associated with the main operation.

In terms of cash flow and returns, ETFs generate both in two ways: (1) through coupon payments on the bonds in their portfolio, and (2) in the form of capital gains when market yields fall. The risk associated with this type of vehicle would mainly be credit risk, since even though the bonds in which it invests are public or investment grade, there is a risk of a decline in their credit ratings.

The following image shows the main characteristics of the fund. As we can see, it currently has 2,917 different fixed-income securities in its portfolio, which indicates that it is a well-diversified fund, although it is less diversified than its benchmark and has the associated risk of deviation from it. The yield to maturity is 4.0%, which reflects the return the fund would obtain if it held all its bonds in its portfolio until maturity.

The average yield maturity shows a value of 2.8 years, which indicates that it is in the short-term range and, consequently, the vehicle has little sensitivity to movements in the long-term curve. The average duration is 2.6, reflecting the vehicle's sensitivity to exchange rates at the short end of the curve. In this case, if the market rose by 1%, the market value would fall by 2.6%.

In this second image, we can see the distribution of the fund by category, highlighting its current strong position in US government bonds. Also noteworthy are its investment grade positions (12.50%) and BBB credit rating tranche (12%). The second chart in this series shows the distribution of bonds by maturity, with more than half of the portfolio in bonds with a maturity of less than 4 years.

Analysis - Macro Fundamental

In this section, we will analyse some macroeconomic indicators in detail in order to offer readers an accurate perspective and sound advice on the vehicle in question.

The first metric to analyse is the Butterfly Heatmap with multiple tenors. For this occasion, we have chosen the tenors (6m-1y-2y, 1y-2y-3y and 2y-3y-5y) based on the maturities of the bonds held in the ETF's portfolio. We constructed the ‘butterfly’ (tranche - 1y-2y-3y) to compare the yield of the belly (2y) with the wings (1y and 3y), to which this fund is extremely sensitive. The greenish/bluish colours indicate when the wings of the figure offer higher yields than the belly (upward curve). Conversely, redder colours indicate a dip in the curve in the middle section.

When the short curve takes on a positive curvature, it means that the extreme points (at the 1-year and 5-year ends) are moving upwards in terms of yield, with greater intensity than the midpoint (around 2–3 years). In this scenario, bonds maturing in approximately three years—where the fund concentrates much of its exposure—see their yields remain stable or even decline compared to the extremes, which translates into a more pronounced appreciation in their price. Since bond prices move inversely to yields, a positive ‘butterfly’ strongly boosts the relative value of those mid-segment bonds, directly benefiting the BSV ETF.

Although the BSV ETF is a passive index fund, its sampling method allows some leeway to optimize its internal composition. In periods where the curve remains at extreme levels (very positive or very negative) for weeks or months, the manager can adjust the selection of bonds within the sample to reduce tracking error or even capture capital gains: for example, by slightly increasing (or reducing) the weight of issues close to the midpoint if he anticipates a shift in the curve, always without deviating from the objective of replicating the benchmark index.

In terms of the recent context, the heat map shows that between April and May 2023, the curve reached positive peaks, driving the vehicle analysed to record increasing relative returns in those months. Subsequently, in autumn-winter 2023, the curve plummeted (very negative values), coinciding with episodes of greater volatility in short bonds and weaker relative performance for the ETF.

During the summer of 2024, there was a new upturn in the positive curve, once again offering the fund opportunities for differential appreciation in its middle section. These ‘butterfly’ cycles help explain why, even with stable general interest rates, the ETF can have periods of outperformance or underperformance depending on how the curve bends or flattens.

In the following metric, we construct a Z-Score that tells us how many standard deviations the constructed curve deviates from its short-term average (in this case, we have used a 60-day window). We mark the extreme values (+2, -2), which reflect unusually high or low curvatures, respectively. More specifically, a Z-score signal of -2 indicates that the curve is at recent historic lows (central part of the face). In these situations, it is most likely that it will gradually reverse, which would give us an advantage in entering medium-term bonds, such as those held by the BSV ETF.

When the z-score exceeds +2, it indicates that the middle section (2 years) is very low or the extremes (1 year, 3 years) are very high. This usually anticipates a reversion to the mean, so the curve will tend to flatten out in the short/medium term. This situation is detrimental to the ETF, as it can translate into relative selling pressure on the ETF due to a decrease in the yield of bonds in the middle range.

Thirdly, we include the metric that relates the S&P 500 to the OAS of BBB-rated bonds. This OAS compares the yield on BBB corporate bonds to that of treasury bonds with the same maturity. This metric helps us analyse the credit risk mentioned in the introduction to this article, as the fund has 12% of its portfolio in BBB-rated corporate bonds.

When the spread increases, the perception of a higher risk of default by the companies issuing these bonds increases, leading to an increase in yield and a decrease in their price, which directly affects the profitability of the fund analysed. In phases where the S&P 500 rises, credit spreads typically narrow, reflecting economic confidence. This tends to positively boost the price of the BSV ETF.

Conclusion

In conclusion, and to wrap up this article, I recommend keeping the vehicle in your portfolio if you already own it, and not buying it if you are currently considering doing so. The butterfly spreads analysed are at a neutral level, meaning that there are no short-term signals indicating the existence of a very favourable movement in the medium term analysed. As for credit spreads, they are currently at historic lows, while the S&P 500 is trading near its historic highs, which reduces the upside potential for credit compression. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Tarsus Pharmaceuticals: Making Big Strides
data.description:
Today, I am putting Tarsus Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (NASDAQ:TARS) in the spotlight for the first time in 2025. When I put out my last article in late 2024, I stated that the early rollout of XDEMVY was nicely exceeding expectations. The stock was trading around $50 a share at that time, and I noted in the piece that I had recently lightened up on my position in this equity due to value. The stock has fallen some 20% since then, and the company has posted a couple of quarterly results from my last analysis. Therefore, it seems a good time to follow up on this commercial-stage biopharma concern. An update analysis follows below.

Tarsus Pharmaceuticals is headquartered in Irvine, CA. The company is focused on the development and commercialization of therapeutic candidates for eye care. It has one FDA-approved product on the market called XDEMVY. This is a novel prescription eye drop designed to treat Demodex blepharitis by targeting and eradicating the root cause of the disease - Demodex mite infestation. It was approved by the FDA in July of 2023. The stock trades just north of $40.00 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of $1.7 billion.

Outside of XDEMVY, Tarsus has somewhat of a sparse pipeline. Tarsus does plan to initiate a Phase 2 trial of TP-04 (lotilaner ophthalmic gel) evaluating it to treat Ocular Rosacea in the second half of this year. There is no current FDA-approved treatment for highly prevalent and underserved eye disease that affects 15 million to 18 million Americans. In addition, Tarsus is scheduled to commence a Phase 2 study of TP-05 (an investigational oral tablet) as a potential treatment of Lyme disease, sometime in 2026.

Recent Results:

Tarsus Pharmaceuticals, Inc. posted Q1 numbers on May 1st. The company delivered GAAP earnings of 64 cents a share. This was down for a $1.01 a share GAAP loss. R&D expenses rose to $14.4 million from $12.1 million in the same period a year ago. SG&A costs rose significantly to $85 million from $51.6 million. The rise was due both to an increase in personnel and marketing costs with an emphasis on direct-to-consumer (DTC) efforts to support the rollout of XDEMVY. Management estimates DTC costs will be between $70 million and $80 million in FY2025 and will go up between $5 million and $10 million in Q2 from Q1.

Revenues rose over 210% to $78.3 million. Net product sales rose over 18% from the previous quarters as 72,000 bottles of XDEMVY were purchased. XDEMVY achieved 90% of covered lives via commercial, Medicare, and Medicaid reimbursement. Bolstered by DTC efforts, leadership projects 85,000 to 90,000 bottles of XDEMVY will be booked in Q2. Inventory of XDEMVY remains steady in Q1 at 2.5 weeks of supply.

Management also is scheduled to meet with authorities to determine a regulatory path forward for XDEMVY in Japan. The company remains on track to garner European regulatory approval in 2027 of a preservative-free formulation of XDEMVY. A marketing application for XDEMVY in China has been accepted.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

The analyst community is universally bullish on the prospects of Tarsus Pharmaceuticals right now. Since first quarter results hit the wires, five analyst firms, including Guggenheim and Oppenheimer, have assigned/reissued Buy ratings on the stock. Price targets proffered range from $72 to $85 a share.

Just over 20% of the outstanding float in the shares is currently held short. Insiders have sold just over $4 million worth of equity collectively so far in 2025. Tarsus Pharmaceuticals ended Q1 with approximately $408 million of cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet after posting a net loss of $25.1 million for the quarter. The company raised some $135 million in proceeds via a secondary offering in the quarter. Tarsus listed approximately $72 million of long-term debt on the 10-K it filed for the quarter.

Conclusion:

Tarsus Pharmaceuticals, Inc. lost 87 cents a share in FY2024 on approximately $183 million worth of revenues in FY2024. The current analyst firm consensus has losses rising to $1.23 a share even as sales soar to $400 million in FY2025. The project profits of 95 cents a share in FY2026 on revenue growth in the mid-40s. They see earnings of four bucks a share on sales growth in the low 30s in FY2027. It is important to note that there is wide variance for both sales and earnings among analyst firms around FY2026 and FY2027.

One has to be impressed with the progress with the rollout of XDEMVY in just less than two years. The stock currently trades at 4.4 times forward revenues - not an unreasonable valuation given the growth trajectory of XDEMVY. Tarsus also has two other mid-stage candidates in its pipeline that have potential.

Given this, I don't believe just over 10 times FY2027E EPS is too much to pay for at least a small starter position in TARS. Obviously, if there wasn't such a wide range of estimates for earnings in FY2027, I would take a much larger position. That said, I can gain a lower entry point and downside risk mitigation by utilizing covered call orders to increase my holdings in TARS.

data.title: S&P Global: High Quality, Fairly Priced
data.description:
Investment thesis

I assign a “Hold” rating to S&P Globa (NYSE: SPGI) for its balance between growth trend and future potential, and its current valuation which seems quite high as it is also the case for its main competitors.

While the company maintains a strong competitive position on the market of financial data providing solutions, global insights and credit ratings, its current valuation appears to be fair and actually leaves not much room for a considerable stock price growth. Moreover, the additional tailwinds generated by the rise of ESG and sustainable financial data, the high margins and steady revenue growth and successful acquisitions could also be balanced by short-term headwinds potentially engendered by a new macroeconomic slowdown or geopolitical tensions affecting the global capital markets.

Company overview

Henry Varnum Poor founded S&P Global in 1860 as the financial information publishing company of the US railroad industry. After a major merger with Standard Statistics in 1941, the company changed its name for Standard & Poor’s. It then joined The McGraw-Hill Companies in 1966, and was finally rebranded as S&P Global more recently in 2016. During its journey, the firm built itself a strong reputation on the credit ratings and financial data providing platforms and is now considered as one of the top three providers with Moody’s and Fitch.

The company thus operates as a provider of credit ratings, but also benchmarks, analytics, and data regarding global capital and commodity markets. Its business is divided into five divisions for more efficiency and clarity between the operations. S&P Global Ratings is the platform on which the ratings assessing the creditworthiness, research and sustainable finance opinions are available for investors and financial institutions. S&P Dow Jones Indices provides index solutions for all asset classes and helps investors with data and analytics sources. S&P Global Commodity Insights serves energy and commodities investors with data and insights on the global commodities markets. S&P Global Market Intelligence is a market data provider and analytics technology platform helping investors access useful resources and make decision more efficiently. And lastly, S&P Global Market Mobility provides insights regarding the automotive industry for business optimization and consumer targeting.

Financial analysis

While we are getting closer to the Q2 2025 results release date set for July 31, 2025, it seems important to remind ourselves of the latest financial situation published for Q1 2025. I gathered the income statement and balance sheet, which will be used to have a clearer view about S&P’s income, structure, and liquidity position.

As shown on the income statement, the total revenues generated by S&P Global during the first quarter 2025 increased by 8% compared to Q1 2024, and almost reached $3.8B. In the meantime, the operating expenses increased by less than 5%, making the operating margin go up to almost 41.7%, a meaningful improvement compared to the 39.7% operating margin realized the year before. After interest, taxes and other income and expenses, the net income attributable to shareholders reach $1.09B, representing a 10% increase compared to Q1 2024. These numbers not only show a positive market trend and the capacity of S&P to increase its revenues, but also the firm’s ability to scale up its activities without losing control of its costs, which improves the overall business profitability and also shows signs of good management.

Regarding its balance sheet, the overall size decreased for the assets (-0.5%) and the liabilities (-2.4%). Only the equity increased by 0.6% to reach almost $33.5B, which represents more than half of the total liabilities and equity. This equity is composed of additional paid-in capital and retained income, to which are subtracted accumulated other comprehensive loss and common stock in treasury. Overall, the structure does not look risky, with a good equity structure and a nonsignificant amount of noncontrolling interests. The cash position decreased by almost 12%, but remains sufficient to cover the short-term debt. The long-term debt is controlled and represents approximately half of the $22B of total liabilities. The firm thus looks safe, with a comfortable cash position providing financial flexibility and investing power, and the structure looks healthy and stable.

Stock valuation

In order to better understand S&P’s position in terms of value compared to its industry average and main competitors, we use the forward PE ratio and compare it with its industry peers. S&P Global currently trades at a forward PE ratio of 36.85, which is apparently far above the sector median of 11.75. In comparison, its main industry peers like Intercontinental Exchange, Moody’s and CME Group respectively trade at forward PE ratios of 33.02, 26.52 and 39.60. S&P’s forward PE is in the upper margin, but still not far from its industry peers, and its forward PE can be considered as normal for the market. However, the high values observed illustrate that the sector might be overestimated by the market and investors’ confidence in this type of company is very high, which could require new investors to remain careful when considering investing in these companies.

To go even further in the valuation process, I ran a DCF valuation allowing us to obtain a rough idea of S&P Global’s intrinsic value. This DCF simulation has been made using a slightly careful approach to avoid any overestimate and to better reflect the company’s value over a 10 year period by taking into account potential slowdowns compared to the current growth trend. This is why the annual growth rate taken into consideration is 10% instead of the 22% calculated based on the average of the last 4 years of activity. The parameters also include a 2.5% perpetual growth rate as well as an 8% discount rate. The DCF output shows a fair price per share of approximately $515, which is very close and can even be considered as equal to the current price of S&P’s stock. Even though investors should remain aware of the fact that this output is only based on assumptions and that it could further be affected by unexpected factors in reality, we understand that S&P’s current stock price is close to its fair price per share expected for a 10 year period of investment. In this situation, investors could thus consider other investments with a better return to integrate their portfolio.

Risks and opportunities to be considered

S&P Global benefits from several strong growth catalysts that continue to nurture its long-term growth. The company holds a leading competitive position in the global financial information ecosystem, followed closely by Moody’s and Fitch. This situation is very profitable, but implies some risks as for one wrong step or mistake degrading its reputation, its well-established competitors would take over the market and benefit from the situation. It thus confirms a strong positioning but also the need to remain careful in the important decisions made to keep the first place.

Also, the recent but long-lasting expansion of ESG and sustainable finance markets represents a key opportunity for S&P Global to increase its income while diversifying its revenue base. Through its Sustainable1 platform, the firm gained a significant competitive advantage by catching the demand from institutional investors and organizations. This key opportunity has been emerging almost a decade ago and is still increasing fast which makes it an important market, attractive for S&P’s activity development in this segment.

Additionally, S&P enjoys a consistent revenue growth and high margins generating a strong profitability for the firm. With its sustainable business model with recurring revenue platform subscriptions, the company has shown its ability to make loyal customers from prospects and build itself a solid cyclical revenue base. The successful external growth operations such as the merger with IHS Markit in 2022 increased this growth effect while broadening the firm’s range of offerings, especially through recently trending sectors like energy and private markets. And with new trending sectors appearing and the technological innovations like artificial intelligence, S&P could improve its platforms and increase its capital efficiency to gain profitability while diversifying its sources of revenues through new platforms and services or acquisitions.

However, these positive trends are balanced by a few major sources of risks that investors should keep in mind. First of all, a renewed macroeconomic slowdown could once more weigh on the global capital markets directly impacting S&P’s ratings and market data segments. As companies would face delayed funding and tighter credit conditions, the budget allocated to market data subscriptions could also be decreased during this time of slowdown or decline, leading to a loss in S&P’s revenues. Considering the impact of a macroeconomic slowdown on M&A activities as well, the company’s segment generating revenue from transaction-oriented information and analytics would also suffer.

Moreover, the ongoing geopolitical situation with rising tensions is also an additional risk weighing on the global markets performances, which then affects S&P’s activities. While the company is globally well-positioned and generates revenues from very different geographical sources, the globalization effect makes every geopolitical tension have an impact at the global scale, and some asset classes can be particularly affected which can be either positive or negative for S&P’s, generating waves of uncertainty and volatility as it was the case with agricultural commodities during the conflicts between Russia and Ukraine.

Conclusion

To summarize, S&P Global remains a high-quality company with strong fundamentals and a solid and resilient structure. Its leading market position gives it a significant advantage, but the firm has to keep it against threatening competitors like Moody’s and Fitch. The firm also benefits from long-term growth catalysts like the ESG and sustainable finance data demand growth and encouraging conditions of the global capital markets. The successful merger operation with IHS Markit and other potential external growth operations to be realized could also enhance this growth in the future and boost the firm’s revenues, while the recent technological innovations could improve S&P’s future performances by being integrated into their platforms and offerings, increasing its capital efficiency and profitability.

On the other hand, short-term risks cannot be ignored. The constant threat of a macroeconomic slowdown or downturn, reduced market activity for M&A, and rising geopolitical tensions are all endangering S&P’s future performances. On top of that, the current valuation shows a fair value per share approximately equal to the current share price, limiting the upside potential on the measured period (10 years).

Given this mixed outlook and the balance between risks and opportunities, I confirm my “Hold” rating on S&P Global. I believe a more favorable entry point could present a better opportunity for long-term investors. The Q2 2025 results expected to be published on July 31, 2025, will probably lead some short-term volatility waves, and investors must remain careful and attentive to any opportunity arising over the next few weeks.

data.title: AutoNation: Weak Balance Sheet Under The Strong Sales
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Tariff barriers have made used cars more expensive and are selling a little faster than otherwise in America. AutoNation (NYSE:AN) benefits from this trend in its sales. But the underlying strength of its sales from various metrics shows weakness and vulnerability lurking. Added with its weak free cash flow and high debt load, the company's high valuation isn't justified.

Company Overview

Autonation, founded in 1996 with headquarters in Fort Lauderdale, Florida, is one of the largest automotive retailers in the United States with 325 new vehicle franchises from 243 stores.

Analysis

AutoNation reported strong sales in the first quarter of 2025 with both its top line and bottom line beating the consensus. However, these upbeat results masked an underlying declining trend in the gross PVR (Price Per Vehicle Retailed). For the Used Car, the gross PVR is at $1,662, about 6.68% higher than the annual average of 2024, but its New Car gross PVR is $2,803, an 8% lower in a similar comparison. The New Car sales are the largest from its revenue, which we will analyze in detail later. Overall, both are still significantly off from their peak, but still above the pre-COVID era.

Furthermore, the margins for both New and Used cars seem to have continued to trend lower. Although the Used Cars have a slight pickup in Q1 at 6.3% from 5.9% previous year's average, it doesn't escape a long-term falling trend since 2008. The New Car margin declined from 5.9% average in 2024 to Q1 '25 at 5.4%. In its Q1 presentation, the company gave a bullish FY 2025 average expectation of the margins for both margins, but it seems to hard to reconcile for us. It expects the FY25's gross PVR for New Car to be $2,611, lower than the Q1 number, but FY margin to rise to 6.6% from Q1's 5.4%. Similarly, it expects Used Car's gross PVR to be $1,617, also lower than Q1, but the FY average margin for these cars to be 8%. We think both margins could be on a continuous decline should the expected FY average gross PVR's be realized.

The revenue mix shows AutoNation is still predominantly reliant on New Car for sales, which contributes 48.8% of the sales but only 16.2% of gross profit. This makes New Cars sales' relative profitability at only 33%, and Used Cars sales are about 31%. This means the company has to make 3 units of New Car sales to contribute to 1 unit of gross profit, similarly for Used Cars. But Used Cars got this level of relative profitability with just a bit more than half of New Car sales. This level of margin is trailing far behind the other two segments. Parts and Service is contributing 46.2% of the gross profit with only 17.2% of the sales, not to mention that Finance and Insurance's outsized profit contribution of 28.4% with only 5.1% of the sales. That means Parts and Services is relatively profitable at 2.7x and Financing is 5.56x. Overall, its largest sales component is the least profitable one.

The divergence in profit margins we show above and the declining trend in gross PVR explain the difference between its revenue and gross profit. With a stable to slightly growing revenue, AutoNation's gross margin has been on a steep decline since 2022. The question is, at what level will its gross profit hold? The average gross margin pre-COVID is around 16.8% and the lowest it has been in the past 20 years is at about 15%; it is currently slightly below 18%. Its gross margin can only decline another 100bps to keep the growth momentum, and in about another 250bps to slip into a bearish outlook.

In addition to the sliding gross profit, AutoNation also has weaker free cash flow, measured both in relation to its EBITDA and Revenue. The company's free cash flow to EBITDA ratio has been negative since mid-last year, and now only falling further to about -25%. Its free cash flow to revenue ratio is also squarely in red at about -1%.

AutoNation's debt load has seen increases in both long term and short term debt. Its earnings were greatly impacted in 2020 but recovered quickly afterwards. As a result, its debt-to-EBITDA ratio stayed around 2x for about two years in 2022-2023 until consistently trending up to close 5.8x now. Its debt-to-equity ratio shows a less distorted picture since its equity is more stable than earnings. Currently, debt-to-equity for the company is at around 3.7x. It has been at such a high level before in 2016 and 2023. From 2016 onward, this ratio was reduced substantially to 1.5x in about 5 years. So the current rise in debt-to-equity ratio is a new trend in the past year and a half.

The recent rise in used car sales has thrust car dealers back into the spotlight, following their previous boom time during the COVID pandemic. Although the determination of the White House to implement the tariff could have lasting impact on the national car sales, the actual trend since last year is much more gentle than the one in 2020-21. If you blink twice, you might even see it as almost flat.

Industry-wise, there are several trends that are upending the traditional retail car sales model, such as online retailers, digitization of car sales and after-sales services, also the mobility-as-service trend from ride-hailing companies will continue to change the trend of sales. The need to upgrade and invest in CapEx will only increase. The higher debt ratio and negative free cash flow will hinder that growth for AutoNation's competitiveness. We think that investors being laser-focused on AutoNation's sales because of the latest market trend is a misnomer. From a big picture point of view, we have a more neutral view on the company growth perspective. It is best that AutoNation uses its current sales strength to pay down its debt and repair its negative free cash flow, which is at one of its lowest levels in the past 15 years. So when the music stops for the used car sale boom, it won't be caught swimming naked.

Financial Overview & Valuation

Currently, AutoNation's EV-to-EBITDA ratio at 10.93x is the middle range of its peers, such as Lithia Motors (LAD), Group 1 Automotive(GPI), and Asbury Automotive(ABG), but it is trending up for the past year or so a little faster than GPI and ABG due to the declining speed of its EBITDA during this time.

However, by the price-to-book ratio, AutoNation is the most expensive. The book value of its equity is eroded by the high debt load. To buy the company at the current price will be paying 3.38x of the total shareholder equity, while most of its competitors are at 1.3-2x. We don't think it is justified, given the declining margin and negative cash flow. We would recommend a Hold for its stock at the current price or higher.

Risks to the Thesis

The risk to the thesis is on the upside. Suppose that the tariff deters many more cars from being imported into the country in the next 12-18 months, then AutoNation could continue to ride the rising tide for a while. And as we have shown, sales don't translate fully into gross profit. AutoNation doesn't have a lot of room to avoid fixing its negative cash flow and high debt. On the other hand, making these efforts could lead to lower earnings in the short term. Either way, its valuation won't become cheap.

Earnings Preview

For the upcoming earnings release, it is likely AutoNation continues to have strong sales of New and Used cars, as the tariff zigzag that happened in Q2 spurred more consumers to make that delayed purchase decision. Its revenue could still beat consensus, and the earnings could either be right on target or slightly better. However, we expect margin and gross PVR to continue to trend lower. It would be interesting to see where its gross profits are. Additionally, strong sales in Q2 could also lay the ground for a fall later in the year as the consumers' demand is pulled ahead due to tariff concerns when inflation really starts to bite. In any case, we see the current boom time as a limited window for the company to address the medium-term problems. In other words, if it's a beat, we would recommend fading it instead of chasing the highs.

Conclusion

Contrasting AutoNation's weak balance sheet with its strong sales, we draw attention to some of it underlying weaknesses of the retailer. Instead of parsing its car sales by gender or age, we think its margin, cash flow and debt load are more important issues to consider. We think its valuation is on the rich side and recommend a hold.

data.title: QuantumScape Corporation: Q2 2025 Earnings Preview
data.description:
Introduction

Recently, the EV value chain is under immense pressure from raw material volatility and tighter capital availability. The traditional lithium-ion companies are ramping up energy density making QuantumScape Corporation (NYSE:QS) breakthrough an heated debate. QS, a leader in the next generation solid state lithium-metal batteries is expected to report its Q2 2025 earnings report on 23rd,July 2025. This report is significant because it might signal if its solid state is finding traction or whether investors’ patience should wear thin.

With the EV adoption accelerating and battery technology becoming a key differentiator, this company’s progress in commercializing its high-energy-density batteries which are fast charging is a point of interest for many investors.

Looking at the 93.64% stock surge YTD beating the S&P 500 6.58% return, I think investor optimism is palpable potentially fueled by recent breakthroughs such as the Cobra separator process.

Nevertheless, the company is still pre-revenue and its profitability path remains speculative. Can QS sustain its current momentum and deliver on its ambitious roadmap?

Consensus Expectations And What To Watch In The Upcoming Report

This being a pre-revenue company, I think its earning focus is not majorly on the traditional financial metrics but operational milestones. The consensus EPS is -$0.2 which is in line with previous quarters with no significant revenue due to the company’s pre-commercialization status.

In the recent past, the company has had mixed results with two EPS misses, one beating and one being in line with estimates. It is important to note that since this is a prerevenue company, its EPS is highly influenced by R&D expenses and this draws my attention to its Cobra separator process which has entered baseline production. At this stage, I expect higher expenses as scaling up comes at a cost and for this reason, I anticipate an EPS miss in Q2.

Contexualizing Q2 2024, its R&D expenses came in at $97.7 million up from $86.5 million YoY and capital expenditure was approximately $18.9 million. This was due its QSE-5 prototype production and Cobra process. From this perspective, I believe scaling up Cobra separator production will have a similar effect pushing up expenses. I am expecting an earnings loss per share between -$0.23 and $0.25.

Nevertheless, QS being yet to register significant financials, the most important aspects in its coming report are not the financial metrics but its milestones towards the commercialization goal. For this reason. The following are the two major areas I think we should pay attention to in this coming quarterly release.

  • Battery performance updates: We should pay a close attention to potential updates on its solid state lithium metal battery developments, particularly the advancement in Cobra separator process. Recently, it was integrated into standard battery production boosting production speed by ~25x and reducing equipment footprint thus paving way for scaled production. This was a significant milestone which saw its stock soar by about 30%. Any positive updates from this end would signal progress towards commercialization and that would lead to positive sentiments sustaining the current momentum.
  • Production and scalability milestones: Investors should pay attention to any potential insights into the timeline and success of the QSE-5 battery production and Cobra’s process readiness which was achieved ahead of schedule. We should focus on CapEx updates and potential partnerships such as the collaboration with PowerCo which includes a $130 million prepayment for licensing. Any meaningful developments in scalability would signify a step closer to commercialization while new partnerships would signify stronger market traction. This is because to me it would be a validation of its technology and market acceptance.

The Bullish Perspective: Looking Beyond Q2 Earnings

While the upcoming quarterly performance is a short-term price catalyst, I think the long-term view matters alongside the anticipated release. I will begin by reflecting on the superior features of this company’s product which would serve as its value proposition in my view. The company’s solid-state lithium metal batteries offer superior performance as seen through different metrics.

To begin with, its anode free design has a density of 844 Wh/L which is significantly higher than conventional lithium ion batteries which is the region of 300Wh/L. Further, it supports fast charging reactions hitting 80% capacity in under 15 minutes as well as retaining 95% capacity after 1,000 cycles equivalent to about 500,000 km of driving. Above all, its ability to operate at temperatures as low as -30 degrees Celsius and the use of noncombustible and nonflammable separator enhance safety by reducing fire risks.

I don’t just look at these features in isolation, I look at them against the market gap because that speaks about the product’s relevance in the current EV market. Looking at the above features, they seem to address two major gaps in the current market. The first gap is limited range and range anxiety. With current consumer concerned about range anxiety and shorter driving ranges, this company’s 844Wh/L being higher than the conventional battery offers a solution to this making it a relevant product in the market.

Secondly, the other gap is slow charging times. With a typical EV car taking about 8 hours to charge from empty to full using a 7kW charging point, and many electric cars taking about 35 minutes to add about 100 miles of range with a 50kW rapid charger, it is evident that this is a major gap which results to inconveniences. Given this company’s fast charging battery in about 15 minutes with a 95% retention after significant millage, I see its product as a major game changer in this industry.

I am looking at the market opportunity here given its superior product. With the global Solid-State battery market projected to grow by a CAGR of 49.4% between 2025 and 2032, the opportunity is vast for this company. With this company developing the first anodeless cell design with superior features underscores its market leadership and positions it better to seize the opportunities in this growing market.

It is estimated that the global EV adoption will rise significantly in the coming years with approximately 40% the global auto market expected to be made of EVs by 2030. Considering that this company’s technology addresses two main gaps (slow charging and limited range) in the EV industry, its market appeal is significant once it commercializes it product.

The Downside

While the Solid battery seems to be the game changer in this industry, QS faces competition from giants like Toyota and Samsung. With Samsung aiming to commercialize its solid battery by 2027, it threatens to erode QS first mover advantage. Of course QS being a startup faces execution risk as scaling from lab to gigafactory is capital intensive. In the absence of concrete OEM contracts QS could be faced with financial challenges hence limited market penetration. However, with its innovative product, I think it’s very likely to win major contracts.

Final Thoughts

Although the long-term outlook is positive, the upcoming quarterly report is very critical because it could impact the market sentiments sigficantly. Currently, this stock is on strong upward momentum.

Should the Q2 report turn out to be strong, I expect this momentum to be sustained. Recent gains such as the 30% surge following Cobra Separator process progress is an indication of how the market is responsive to the company’s milestone. This shows why any major announcements in the Q2 report could move the market significantly. With a beta of almost 2, investors should brace for volatility following quarterly release. From a long-term perspective, I think this company’s innovation is very relevant in the dynamic and growing EV industry. As such, I would recommend buying this stock in dips and holding in the long run.

Given the company’s recent breakthrough with Cobra separator process which was achieved earlier than anticipated, I believe the company is on track to send its QSE-5 B1 samples this year to its customers for field testing next year in line with their plan.

In conclusion, QS is on the right path as hinted by its recent technological breakthrough which has sparked market optimism, although I expect its EPS to fall below expectations due high costs associated with ramping up production, I anticipate positive updates on key milestone if the current trajectory is anything to go by. After the earnings report, I will offer a comprehensive follow-up coverage on the stock where I will dive deeper into what the report shall contain and what it implies to the company and us as investors.

data.title: Orla Mining: A Transformational Start To The Year
data.description:
All figures are in United States Dollars unless otherwise noted. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day. UG = Underground. MTPA = million tonnes per annum. FS/DFS = Definitive Feasibility Study.

Q1 Production & Sales

Orla Mining released its Q1-25 results, reporting record quarterly production of ~47,800 ounces of gold and sales of ~46,400 ounces of gold. This represented a 44% increase in output from the year-ago period, helped by another solid quarter out of Camino Rojo (~30,000 ounces produced at $845/oz AISC), and partial contribution from its new Musselwhite Mine in Ontario. The latter closed at the end of February and produced ~17,800 ounces in March, with ~104,300 tonnes processed at 5.55 G/T of gold.

Importantly, and unlike several producers that have blown out their share counts to acquire assets in this cycle, Orla added this mid-scale Canadian gold asset with no upfront share dilution and modest dilution from warrants/convertible debt (~18% dilution), translating to meaningful growth in per share metrics. In fact, production per share was up nearly 50% in just a partial quarter of contribution from Musselwhite, with production per share set to more than double in 2025, with further growth in 2026 from a full year of production from Musselwhite at planned higher grades (6.8+ G/T of gold).

Hence, this has been a transformational start to the year for Orla, to say the least. Plus, these production per share metrics do not include the South Carlin Complex, an above-average grade heap leach asset with immense exploration potential (oxide and sulfide) along the highly productive Carlin Trend, home to a ~1.2 million ounce gold producer, the Carlin Complex, with a ~34 million ounce resource base. This is not meant to suggest that Orla is sitting on any 10+ million ounce elephant-scale deposits at its South Carlin Complex, but it's certainly in the right neighborhood for key discoveries and there's evidence of high-grade and high-margin oxide material here (with historic hits like ~126.2 meters at 3.95 G/T of gold), and an abundance of low to mid-grade oxide material that should be able to easily support a 15-year mine life vs. the ~8 year LOMP currently.

As for Orla's progress vs. annual guidance, the company has produced ~125,600 ounces year-to-date, or 43.3% of planned production, with what should be two ~80,000 ounce production quarters on deck to help it reach its 280,000 to 300,000 ounce guidance. And if history is any guide, Orla will deliver on this guidance and come in closer to 300,000 ounces for the year, with an incredible track record to date of beating its guidance midpoint, being one of the few companies that has a habit of massively over-delivering on promises.

Financial Results

Moving to its financial results, Orla reported quarterly revenue of ~$140.7 million (+109% year-over-year) and solid cash flow generation, with the company set to generate upwards of $200 million in free cash flow this year. This is despite significant investments at Musselwhite (~$108 million in sustaining capital and capitalized exploration), and an aggressive exploration program across the portfolio, with ~$43 million in exploration/development expenses across its three sites.

As for Orla stacks up vs. peers, we can see that it's been one of the most consistent companies in its junior/mid-tier peer group from a free cash flow generation standpoint despite being one of the smallest (just over 100,000 ounces/year in the 2021-2024 period), but free cash flow will take a material step higher in 2025, while free cash flow per share should also hit record highs above US$0.53/share on a diluted basis. This is important because while there's no shortage of producers reporting record headline results, their per share figures leave a lot to be desired in most cases, with Coeur Mining (CDE) being one clear example where we've seen free cash flow per share tumble over 80% since 2011 levels even in a record year for gold prices.

In terms of Orla's balance sheet, it's shifted from a strong net cash position (Q4-24: ~$160 million in net cash), to a net debt position, with over $260 million in net debt as of the end of Q1-25. This is related to the purchase of the Musselwhite acquisition, with a fully drawn $250 million credit facility ($100 million term loan, $150 million under existing RCF), and $200 million in unsecured convertible debt (4.5%, maturing in 2030), or $450 million in new debt against its ~$184 million cash position. Fortunately, the gold price has been working in Orla's favor, and the company will be able to quickly de-lever and should exit 2026 back in a net cash position, even under more conservative gold price assumptions.

Funding of the Musselwhite transaction also included a $360 million gold pre-pay on gold forward prices averaging $2,834/oz, with 36 equal monthly deliveries from March 2025 to February 2028 ~4,025 ounces of gold.

Costs & Margins

Looking at Orla's costs and margins, Orla reported Q1-25 AISC of $845/oz based on its Camino Rojo Mine only (does not include Musselwhite), a 7% decline from the year-ago period. The improvement in AISC was largely related to significantly lower sustaining capital spend in the period (~$0.5 million vs. ~$5.0 million) and a much weaker Mexican Peso, partially offset by fewer ounces of gold sold at Camino Rojo. Lower sustaining capital was related to easy comps vs. the higher sustaining capital in Q1-24 related to the construction of Phase 2 of its heap leach pad and higher capitalized exploration.

Overall, these costs were in line with its revised guidance midpoint of $850/oz (Camino Rojo only), benefiting from the weaker Mexican Peso and lower sustaining capital, with just $0.5 million of a planned $5.0 million (revised down from $10.0 million) spent in Q1-25. As for margins, AISC margins surged to $2,070/oz (71.0%), up from $1,150/oz in the year-ago period. This was primarily related to its record average realized gold price of $2,915/oz, in addition to abnormally low sustaining capital in Q1-25 that further benefited margins.

That said, we will see a move higher in AISC starting in Q2-25 at Camino Rojo, impacted by higher royalties (gold-price related), the sharp reversal in the Mexican Peso which has strengthened in Q2-25, and catch-up spend in sustaining capital. As for company-wide AISC, I wouldn't be surprised to see it come in above $1,425/oz in Q2-25, with this obviously largely because of the higher-cost Musselwhite operation where costs have been guided for $1,650/oz at the mid-point in the April to December period. Looking at the chart below, this will de-throne Orla as one of the sector's lowest-cost producers, with full-year 2025 AISC likely to come in above $1,400/oz.

While the higher cost profile that places it closer to the industry average (~$1,600/oz) may turn off some investors, it's important to point out that this deal is truly transformational and a brilliant move by the company even if it has pushed its all-in sustaining costs over 65% higher (~$850/oz ---> $1,400/oz+). This is because it gives Orla scale and it de-risks the company with improved diversification from a net asset value and cash flow standpoint. Plus, it paves a path to being a tri-asset producer in 2028, with less focus on a jurisdiction that has lost some ground in terms of investment attractiveness (Mexico), and the bulk of production/cash flow coming from Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions (Ontario, Canada and Nevada, USA).

Most importantly, and as we'll dig into below, Orla paid a very reasonable price for this asset ($850 million including contingent consideration), given that this is an already producing asset with just shy of 2.0 million meters drilled on the property, a 1.5 MTPA plant, a twin decline and newly commissioned conveyor/material handling system under Newmont's (NEM) ownership, all on a ~65,000 hectare land package north of Thunder Bay, Ontario.
